paragraphs.body: A language that I speak that I love is the language of volleyball. Any sport lingo to me feels like its own separate language and growing up I played a lot of different sports. I watched a lot of sports of my family, the main one being American football.

annotations.comment: Don't forget to develop an intentional introduction form from the options listed on our Canvas site!
annotations.context_left: of different sports. I watched a lot of sports of my family, the main one being American football.

paragraphs.body: I truly didn’t understand the difference between understanding the language as a player and as just a viewer of the game. Not until I started playing volleyball and that sport took over my life. For the past four years I have been playing. It started off with me just watching NCAA games and wanting to be one of those girls. Then I started watching YouTube videos and trying to teach myself.

annotations.text: It started off with me just watching NCAA games and wanting to be one of those girls.
annotations.comment: why, specifically? Let's talk about why volleyball draws you.

paragraphs.body: Even with the terminology videos and the words being used in the YouTube channels I was watching like Koko’s Volley; I did not really understand until I went to my first volleyball clinic and started saying those words randomly. I used them incorrectly. I knew I was using them incorrectly when people would ask me to repeat myself or just tell me that is not what I mean to say. Thats how I grew and understood the language better. By interacting with other players who knew the language better than me at the time.

annotations.text: Even with the terminology videos and the words being used in the YouTube channels I was watching like Koko’s Volley; I did not really understand until I went to my first volleyball clinic and started saying those words randomly. I used them incorrectly.
annotations.comment: I wonder if this idea might be more effective if you developed it in narrative? But, more importantly, what did your misunderstandings reveal for the purposes of this essay?

paragraphs.body: Volleyball feels like a very secretive language. One I only use around coaches, players, and parents familiar with volleyball. Usually these were parents who had daughters playing, or just really liked watching the sport and knew a heavy amount of the game rules.

I almost forget that the volleyball world is a separate world because the places in which I am speaking this language are when I’m surrounded by others who speak it as well. It starts sounding very normal. Especially on the court while I am playing, with my teammates, it comes out more fluently than regular English. Like I will start screaming some abbreviated phrases that would make no sense in other context. For example, I would scream “TIP, TIP, TIP” when I notice the setter on the other side is about to setter dump or “cover deep” when we are in receive and have to cover the back row or “FOUR FOUR” when I’m asking my setter to set me that specific set so I can get an outside shot that I think will win us that point. But that context does not need to be said to the others in the volleyball world, so I forget it must be said to people who are not. Anytime someone asks I always find it a bit shocking until I remember they do not play volleyball like I do and they are not constantly using that type of language. Why do I find it a bit shocking? I do not know. Shocking might not be the word, rather surprising because it is a reality check for me that not everyone is as heavily involved in volleyball as I am.

annotations.text: Shocking might not be the word, rather surprising because it is a reality check for me that not everyone is as heavily involved in volleyball as I am.
annotations.comment: does this experience of insiderness vs. outsiderness occur in other parts of your life as well?

paragraphs.body: I know especially when I am talking to new friends who do not play that sport, I must break down the context, the rules, and the entire game. That also goes for some family members who are not in my immediate circle who watch my games or at least talk with me about volleyball.

That why I enjoy the volleyball spaces so much. At every team tryout I have been to in the last four years, I have easily connected with other players with our volleyball language. Most of the time I give them a compliment on a play of theirs and I know they immediately understand me. It builds a sense of community and unity. That is why I am so attracted to the sport.

annotations.text: It builds a sense of community and unity.
annotations.context_right: That is why I am so

paragraphs.body: The values I have learned from team sports, especially volleyball, have shaped the person I am today. I appreciate my parents having me participate in team sports early on in life because it taught me valuable lessons on how to interact with people and how to make connections with people. It taught me how to be mindful of other people’s emotions. Being able to put myself in their shoes and be more considerate and empathetic to their situation. That was a life lesson so necessary to understand to have effective communication with others. Especially in arguments.

annotations.text: values I have learned from team sports, especially volleyball, have shaped the person I am today. I appreciate my parents having me participate in team sports early on in life because it taught me valuable lessons on how to interact with people and how to make connections with people. It taught me how to be mindful of other people’s emotions. Being able to put myself in their shoes and be more considerate and empathetic to their situation. That was a life lesson so necessary to understand to have effective communication with others. Especially in arguments.
annotations.comment: These are potentially meaningful ideas, but--for the purpose of an essay with one focused central idea, they're new and different. Let's discuss what's _really_ at the heart of this essay for you.

paragraphs.body: Teamwork also taught the lesson of how to work with others. How to compromise with someone else. How to let go of needing to be in control in every situation and step back and consider working with someone else. Without those lessons, I would find myself difficult to reason with, difficult to work with, difficult to find common ground or solution with. Which is not the type of person I wanted to grow up and become.

annotations.text: Without those lessons, I would find myself difficult to reason with, difficult to work with, difficult to find common ground or solution with.
annotations.comment: This is a repetition of idea. You're talking, again, about the value of teamwork beyond the gym, here. Let's explore :)

paragraphs.body: Being able to put my competitive energy into the game gave me an outlet to relax. Nothing feels better than winning and knowing that you earned that win. I loved the discipline of working hard to get better at the game. I was very intense with that. I watch what I eat, I watch how much exercise I am doing, and I watch how my body is feeling since I do not want to overexert myself. There have be countless times when I over did it and hurt myself. That taught me a lesson and finally made me understand the value of having my body and the fact that I only have one body. For the rest of my life. So, I must take care of it; I could not keep sacrificing myself for the team or the win. I had the issue in the past where I had teammates not putting in as much effort or not as much care into our games and our team. Nothing upset me more. I was captain and used volleyball language to confront them. To raise my concerns. They understood how I felt, and I was able to get more context on why they were treating the sport the way they were. Without the calm approach and conversational skills, I doubt the conversations would have had such a good outcome.

annotations.text: Without the calm approach and conversational skills, I doubt the conversations would have had such a good outcome.
annotations.comment: This idea is potentially related to the earlier ideas I highlighted about the value of volleyball beyond the gym.

annotations.text: I loved the discipline of working hard to get better at the game. I was very intense with that. I watch what I eat, I watch how much exercise I am doing, and I watch how my body is feeling since I do not want to overexert myself. There have be countless times when I over did it and hurt myself. That taught me a lesson and finally made me understand the value of having my body and the fact that I only have one body. For the rest of my life.
annotations.comment: Another new idea! You have lots to work with. Let's see where you want to go with this essay.

paragraphs.body: I still hate confrontation but due to volleyball I can say I have gotten better. Due to sports in general, I know I am better at conversation and discipline. Yes, this has created a reliance on having a sport in my life because I need that outlet, but I am grateful for all the beliefs and values that the sport has installed in me. They are positive traits that make me a good person and the person I am now. Someone who I imagine is closer to who I want to become.

annotations.comment: Don't forget to write an intentional intro and conclusion form that echo each other. This feels more like a summary conclusion more suited for a 5-paragraph essay.
annotations.context_left: me a good person and the person I am now. Someone who I imagine is closer to who I want to become.
